title: Devilled Eggs
subtitle: Hard-boiled eggs filled with a smooth yolk mixture seasoned with mustard and cayenne
excerpt: Devilled eggs are one of the simplest party foods: halved hard-boiled eggs with the yolk blended smooth with mayonnaise, mustard, and cayenne, then piped or spooned back into the white. The results depend on the yolk being cooked without overcooking, and the filling being well-seasoned.
sourceNotes: British and European tradition. Stuffed eggs are documented from at least the medieval period in European cookbooks. The specific term 'devilled' for hot or piquant preparations appears in British cookbooks from the 18th century. The modern party version became standard in British and American entertaining in the mid-20th century.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale freely; the recipe doubles and triples without any changes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 4 hours ahead. Cover and refrigerate. Fill at the last minute if possible for the best appearance.

glossaryTerms.definition: To cook eggs fully submerged in simmering water for 10 to 12 minutes until both white and yolk are completely set. Cooling immediately in cold water stops the cooking and prevents the grey-green ring that forms around the yolk when eggs are overcooked.

content.text: Devilled eggs succeed on three things: the yolk cooked to a clean, powdery set; the filling blended smooth rather than simply mashed; and the seasoning pushed further than seems necessary. Cold food needs strong seasoning. Taste the filling before it goes back in and add more mustard, salt, or cayenne than you think is right.

items.prepNote: a pinch, plus more to garnish

content.text: Lower the {{eggs}} eggs gently into a pan of simmering water.
for 10 minutes, then transfer immediately to a bowl of cold water and leave for 5 minutes. Peel carefully.
Halve each egg lengthways. Pop the yolks into a bowl. Place the whites on a serving plate, cut-side up.

Mash the yolks with a fork until no lumps remain, then beat in the {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, the mustard, lemon juice, and a pinch of cayenne. Season generously with salt and white pepper. The filling should be smooth and hold its shape when dropped from a spoon.
Spoon or pipe the filling back into the egg whites, mounding it slightly. Dust with a pinch of smoked paprika and cayenne. Serve chilled.

items.cause: Eggs were overcooked or not cooled quickly enough.
items.fix: 10 minutes from the moment they enter simmering water is sufficient. Plunge into cold water immediately and leave for at least 5 minutes. The ring is harmless but signals that the yolk will be drier and more sulphurous than optimal.

items.cause: Yolks not mashed fine enough before the mayonnaise was added.
items.fix: Mash the yolks to a powder before adding the mayonnaise. A fine-mesh sieve pressed through with the back of a spoon produces the smoothest result.

items.symptom: Filling slides out of the white
items.cause: Too loose, or assembled too far in advance and the filling settled.
items.fix: Use enough mayonnaise for a thick, pipeable consistency. If serving at a party, fill just before serving.

content.text: Stuffed eggs appear in European cookbooks from the medieval period, seasoned with herbs, cheese, or spices. The devilled version uses cayenne, mustard, and Worcestershire sauce, and is documented in British cookbooks from the 18th century. The cold party version became standard in British and American entertaining in the 1950s and has remained a reliable buffet item since. The term devilled is applied to other dishes in this collection: devilled kidneys and devilled mackerel follow the same principle of hot, pungent seasoning.
